sentences.text: My name maiden name is Jukin, U.
Yeah, I don't know if I actually [LAUGHS] know how to spell it.
I wrote everything down, so I should have it next.
But I-- They're all the same.
And I have to put on my glasses.
I really can't remember very well.
Yes, I have the one brother.
Oh, well, his name was Mendel.
I can't really-- I can't really tell you.
Maybe a year and a half or two years or maybe-- exactly, I don't know.
My--my oldest sister's was-- was Tone.
Let me spell it to you.
I'll put that down so I can-- [SIGHS] [INAUDIBLE] I don't have the right thing.
I misplaced my little thing what I made.
Yeah, but-- I really can't tell you exactly because--
And except-- I still don't know, I mean, I don't exactly how-- when she was born, what year.
But around the same time-- something like two.
It's hard for me to tell.
No, after-- after Malka, I came.
And then came my little sister, Bunia.
We had-- all the Jewish grew up-- people lived in center of the town.
Everybody knew almost everybody because it was a small area.
That's what I think as much as I can remember.
Had a lot of friends-- school friends, neighbors, things like that-- children.
And my whole family lived there-- my mother's and my father's.
It was-- to me, it looked big.
[LAUGHS] But there was Polish people who lived in Stepan, and Ukrainian people lived in Stepan.
Some even German people lived in Stepan.
And I really can't tell you how many people--
The size of the Jewish community, like I said before, was the center of the city and a little bit a few streets out-- out of the center of the city.
It was-- we had a very big shul, but it had rooms like smaller-- the smaller shul and then the big sanctuary and then this.
We had the shul for the women, the shul for the men, the smaller-- the smaller minyans, you know, like I said, in that shul.
And, like I said before, the big center of the-- which my father davened there.
I think we were not like orthodox like you see here with beards and things like that.
He-- he went to shul three times a day every day.
He was davening by the amud, you know and they-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]
And is-- was-- I would say religious.
We-- the whole family was religious.
We kept the Shabbat, all the holidays.
There was no such a thing like taking a car of something.
We were not even allowed to bring a piece of paper or the-- or anything, write.
But at the-- most of-- one time-- one-- there was times that we had to be in school, but I did not write.
I can't say most, but the bigger-- bigger, but--
That's what I think because the younger people maybe were not so.
[LAUGHS] But that's what I, to my knowledge, I think that's what it [? was. ?]
Yes, we had a Hebrew school by the name-- they called it the-- oh, forgot already.
Just-- it was just on my tongue.
And they-- the children-- that-- that was a private school, and you had to pay for the education.
And so we had a small-- Tarbut was the name.
I don't think so because I've never-- I was never-- I never went to that school so I really don't know.
Where did-- where did you go to school?
And did you get a Jewish education like in the afternoon?
Yeah, we had a man who lived in our house, and he was teaching us Hebrew, Jewish, how to write Jewish, how to read Hebrew, a little davening, you know.
No, I only remember only this one.
And my brother went to, I think-- was he going Cheder or not?
Yeah, my father was a butcher, and he had a kosher butcher shop.
And there were stores that you-- stores that you could buy kosher meat.
Our house was-- was considered large.
It was two bedrooms, large size, big living room, nice-sized kitchen.
Then we had a big garden.
We had-- I couldn't-- it was considered as a nice size house.
Also, my father's butcher shop was with the-- it's at the house, sort of like.
Well, he brought the animals at the outside, you know, the Gentile people were selling the animals.
They were raising animals that-- for selling them.
And then, we had the slaughter house, which a shochet came, and they slaughtered the-- the kosher meats.
Outside a little bit was, in the school, Polish, Ukrainian.
But the main language was, in my parents' house, Jewish.
You know, I was too young to know that anti-Semitism is something, you know-- there's such a thing like anti-Semitism.
So I really-- I know-- I knew one thing that when we were in school, everybody stick to their own-- own--
We were-- we were the Jewish kids were with the Jewish kids.
The Gentile kids were with the Gentile.
Even the Ukrainian and the Polish didn't mix.
So everybody stuck to their own thing, you know.
And in class-- this was after the class-- in class, we sat together, mixed.
Jewish people from other nationalities, you know, I don't really know.
But what I meant nationalities was like Polish, Ukrainian people, and the-- also, not too many families but a few German.
And we used to call them the false Deutschen.
Because they were not in their own country like-- like the-- what do we-- the-- like here, they called us the-- what do you call it?
And the Russian, I really don't remember them.
So that's what I-- the way I saw it.
I mean, maybe it was different, but that's the way I saw it, yeah.
Not often, but it happened, yeah.
My usual life in that year was-- I can only tell you the time when [LAUGHS] I start to go to [? school. ?] We woke up in the morning, and we got dressed.
And we went out to school, 8 o'clock.
We had to go to school.
And before school we played with children.
And I played with children in there.
And then, we came home from school, and it was dinner.
The school day was like probably five hours or six hours.
Yeah, yeah, about two or three o'clock so.
No, I didn't go to Hebrew school.
The tutor was in my house, my parents' house.
So then I made-- had homework and did all of that whatever I ever had.
And then we played a little.
I played with my friends outside a little.
And that's just normal like here.
I believe I was 13 years or so.
[SIGHS] Because we hoped-- we were-- we were not very poor, but we were not rich.
But my father knew how to daven.
He knew how to pray and shiva.
My mother was never in-- she didn't go to school.
She didn't know how to read.
And so we used to get, like, you know, from my-- my sister, especially, from a friend or so.
She-- they had a newspaper, so she took-- you know, she borrowed it, so.
And every news, you know what-- what happened.
We didn't have radios in the house.
And we didn't have anything else.
But was-- news, they always put them outside in the main streets, you know, like in the center of the city on poles like they still do it in Israel now--
--and announce so we knew what's going on.
And some people-- I remember one family, I think, was-- was either rich, and they had the radios-- a radio.
Everybody went to the house to listen to the news.
We didn't know what a telephone meant-- meant really-- a telephone.
It was Jewish, a Jewish paper.
No, not until [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '40, 1940, I think when they came.
I didn't, but my parents did because of everybody was interested to know what's going on.
Not-- not-- not to my knowledge.
Maybe somebody else did, but I, myself, as a child I did not feel that there was any danger until the German came in.
But up to then, I didn't-- I didn't-- I didn't know anything.
Well, first of all, they-- when they-- when they came in-- before they came in, the Russian came into our town.
In 1913-- in 1939 the Russian came in.
And they stayed in our-- in my area for-- and up until the German came.
So we, like-- it was, like, a year or maybe a year and a half, we were-- well, the Russian-- and then the German came.
And we-- Let's talk about the Russian occupation a little bit.
You say they stayed in Stepan?
The Russian were-- they were the Russian government.
Yeah, what happened-- what I did to my life, [LAUGHS] I switched from public school to-- to-- to-- to a Hebrew school.
And-- and we were learning Russian.
No, I could have gone to the public school.
It was changed from Polish to Russian.
But I felt as the Jewish person, I felt I wanted to be in the Jewish school.
I couldn't do that before because we could not afford it.
So because-- but they also learned-- we also-- it was only, not only Jewish that was allowed, we went-- we learned Russian, we were learned Ukrainian.
This book-- I-- we learned a few languages that we will-- we knew, you know.
It was not just like here, English.
It was more than one language--
I think all my friends switched, so I switched.
Well, they was-- they were running-- that was the government.
And what we did, we did-- we had to go-- there wasn't enough food so we had to go and stay in line for everything that they-- that we needed-- sugar, bread.
It wasn't like going into the store and shop like it was before.
Always-- anything-- there was every day they had something else in the store for-- not-- I'm not talking about clothing.
Well, I assume this was like in Russia.
That's the way it was in Russia at that time too.
The Communist party can, you know, they wanted us to convert to the party, you know, to believe it to be Communist and things like that.
Well, because all of a sudden, like, say before we find out the war has break-- was broke out, everybody around went out to shop, shop, shop because we didn't know how long it's going to last.
So, and then they came in.
We didn't have-- we had to stay in line for everything.
I don't-- I don't really remember.
Well, that was a change because the-- in-- before the Russian came in and the Polish occupation was, everybody got-- had a home business.
And when they came, of course, they made every business their own-- that it belonged to the-- to the Communists, to the Russians, I mean, to the government.
And that's what-- that's what changed.
And-- and otherwise, I don't think it changed much more than that, you know.
We-- Everybody had to work the government.
Yes, that's when the-- the Russians were-- were-- left the-- the-- my town, Stepan, and the German came, we stayed a little-- a very short time in our own homes.
And then they created the ghetto.
I think it was the-- the late '40s or early '41.
The ghetto was-- was located where the shul was, where the temple was.
And they put fence all around, very high fence.
And it was, like, about, I would say, about two, three small streets.
Yeah, all the Jewish people from town and from out of town, like, some close, really just then a few families were brought in.
Well, the synagogue became-- no-- no more synagogue.

They keep-- in the synagogue, I think, they were keeping the potatoes and they cooked the food there because we used to get-- we-- every day we went for-- for our, our lunch or our dinner or whatever.
And they gave us soup everyday.
This-- so everyone had a-- a little bit of soup.
What we did, we came home.
And it was all-- the potatoes always were frozen.
So we ate was never, like, sweet.
When they are frozen, they are sweet, and they smelled.
But for us, we didn't have anything else to eat.
The Jewish people actually cooked them.
They put the people to work.
But they cooked what they had, what they gave-- what they gave them to cook.
No, I was in the ghetto.
We had to leave our house, and we went into the ghetto.
And they divided the ghetto in tow halves.
The men went-- was-- lived in one half and the women and children in the other half side.
They-- if there was a boy who became 14 years old, he had to go to the men's side.
And I could only see my father-- there was also a fence between the two sides.
And the only time I went to see my father, I sneaked in.
Or some-- once in a while, they let you in or they let them out to see the family.
In my house-- in the house I lived?
The house had two rooms and a kitchen.
My-- There was-- there was nothing to cook unless we-- we sneaked out of the ghetto and were running going to Polish or Ukrainian people begging for some food.
Till they gave us some of the-- yeah.
I was the only one in the family.
